food_subgroup: Deep-fried fermented wheat batter spirals soaked in sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Jalebi. Nearest parent is a fried confectionery in sugar syrup. A new concept for jalebi-style fermented wheat spiral sweets is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried wheat flour confection in sugar syrup, Indian (closest parent)

main_ingredients: all-purpose flour (maida) or refined wheat flour

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of jalebi is 4 pieces (approximately 100g total) — bright orange-yellow, crispy, spiral-shaped fried sweets that are dripping with absorbed sugar syrup, served warm.

nutrient_source.title: Jalebi — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11100011; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F11100011 — Jalebi, standard deep-fried fermented wheat batter in sugar syrup preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — jalebi GI very high due to the predominantly sugar syrup composition; almost entirely rapidly-absorbed simple sugars.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 48mg per 100g serving — very low; jalebi is essentially sugar, oil, and flour with minimal salt.
hypertension.guidance: From a sodium standpoint, jalebi is safe for hypertensive patients. The primary health concern is the very high sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high GI (~82) and very high sugar content (52g/100g); glycaemic load per 100g serving is approximately 54 — extremely high. Jalebi is among the most glycaemically dangerous foods.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients must avoid jalebi completely or limit to 1 small piece (15–20g) on rare occasions under glucose monitoring. The sugar syrup content is essentially pure rapidly-absorbed sucrose.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sugar content (52g/100g) promotes inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); deep frying adds pro-inflammatory omega-6 fats.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit jalebi — the combination of extremely high sugar and deep-frying is among the most pro-inflammatory dietary patterns. Occasional small piece on festive occasions only.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jalebi is one of India's most vibrant street food sweets — a fermented wheat flour batter piped through a cloth or squeeze bottle in spiral shapes directly into hot oil, deep-fried until crispy, then immediately immersed in warm sugar syrup flavoured with saffron and cardamom until they absorb the syrup and become glisteningly orange and syrup-saturated. The contrast between the crispy exterior and syrup-filled interior is the defining eating experience. Very high in sugar — the syrup penetrates the fried dough completely. In Singapore, jalebi is sold at Indian sweet shops and during Deepavali celebrations.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F11100011 — Jalebi, standard preparation.
notes: Contains gluten (wheat flour) and dairy (yogurt at trace fermentation level). Not suitable for coeliac or gluten-sensitive patients. Cholesterol approximately 2mg per 100g from trace yogurt. The very high sugar (52g/100g) is primarily from sugar syrup absorption. Very low water content (16.5%) reflects the high sugar and fat density. Imarti (made with urad dal instead of wheat) is a gluten-free variant with similar caloric profile.
